title: Staffordshire oatcakes
subtitle: Yeasted oat-and-flour griddle pancakes from the Midlands
excerpt: Staffordshire oatcakes are thin, yeasted pancakes made from oatmeal and flour, cooked on a dry griddle until lacy and browned on both sides. They are a savoury bread-substitute, not a biscuit. Serve fresh from the griddle wrapped around bacon, cheese, or a fried egg.
sourceNotes: Staffordshire oatcakes are a regional English food with documented roots in the Potteries towns of North Staffordshire, particularly Stoke-on-Trent, where they were sold from oatcake shops and hawked door-to-door from the nineteenth century onwards. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932), records several regional oatcake recipes including the Staffordshire type made from a yeast-batter of oats and flour. The recipe here follows the traditional method: fine oatmeal and plain flour, a yeast batter left to ferment, cooked on an unoiled iron griddle.

recipe.freezeNotes: Cool completely, stack with baking paper between each oatcake, freeze in a bag. Reheat in a dry pan for 2 minutes each side or toast from frozen.
recipe.batchNotes: The batter doubles easily. Cook in batches as the griddle heats.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The batter can ferment in the fridge overnight. Bring to room temperature for 20 minutes before cooking.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: Cooked on a medium-hot griddle or heavy frying pan, no oil. The griddle is ready when a drop of water skitters across the surface.

glossaryTerms.definition: A liquid dough left to ferment at room temperature before cooking. Unlike bread doughs, an oatcake batter is thin enough to pour; the yeast is included to develop flavour and some lightness rather than to create a risen structure. An hour of fermentation at 20°C is enough; over-fermented batter has a sharp, acidic edge.

content.text: Staffordshire oatcakes occupy a space between a griddle pancake and a flatbread. They are made from a thin

of oatmeal and plain flour, cooked on a dry iron griddle until the top sets and the underside is mottled brown. In the Potteries towns where they originated, oatcake shops open early and sell them warm for breakfast; the tradition is still alive in Stoke-on-Trent. Outside the region, they are largely unknown, which is a pity. They have more flavour than a wheat pancake and keep well for a day.

attrs.prepNote: blended to a fine meal in a food processor, or buy fine oatmeal

content.text: Whisk together the oatmeal, flour, yeast, and salt in a large bowl. Pour in the warm milk and water while whisking, until you have a thin, smooth batter with no lumps. Cover with a plate and leave at room temperature for 1 hour; small bubbles will appear on the surface as the yeast gets to work.

Heat a griddle pan or heavy frying pan over medium heat, without any fat. The pan is ready when a drop of water skitters across the surface and evaporates in under a second. Pour a small ladleful (about 80 to 100 ml) of batter onto the pan and swirl gently to spread it to roughly 20 cm across. Cook for 2 to 3 minutes until the surface is set and the underside is mottled with brown patches. Flip carefully and cook for a further 1 to 2 minutes until the second side is browned. Stack on a warm plate while you cook the rest. Serve warm, with the oatcake folded or rolled around bacon, cheese, or eggs.
